← Previous: 2026-04-10 Issue #2

Paper #1: Statistical Signatures of Majorana Zero Modes in Disordered Topological Superconductor Antidot Vortices ​

Authors: Zhibo Ren, Jukka I. Väyrynen
Affiliations: Purdue University
arXiv: 2604.11692 [cond-mat.supr-con]
Date: April 14, 2026
Domain: superconductivity, quantum-info
Why collected: A novel spatial-statistics criterion to distinguish Majorana zero modes from trivial CdGM states in disordered vortex cores via STM

Summary ​

This work develops a random matrix theory (RMT) framework to study how disorder affects Majorana zero modes (MZMs) and Caroli-de Gennes-Matricon (CdGM) states in an antidot-pinned vortex on a 3D topological insulator-superconductor platform. The key finding is that the variance of the MZM probability density is exactly twice that of CdGM states in the large-system limit, because the MZM wavefunction is real (GOE statistics) while CdGM states are complex (GUE statistics). This translates into an inverse participation ratio (IPR) ratio of 4/3, providing a disorder-invariant spatial signature accessible to scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) beyond the conventional zero-bias conductance peak.

Core Concepts ​

  • Antidot-Pinned Vortex: A superconducting antidot geometry created by locally removing the superconductor in a TI-SC heterostructure. Unlike conventional Abrikosov vortices, the vortex position is spatially fixed and can host a single MZM at small magnetic fields.

  • Caroli-de Gennes-Matricon (CdGM) States: Low-energy bound states generically present in vortex cores. They lack topological protection and respond to disorder in both energy and wavefunction.

  • RMT Low-Energy Hamiltonian: The effective Hamiltonian projected onto the bound-state subspace is modeled as a (2N+1)-dimensional antisymmetric random matrix HR:

    H=∑i,j=12N+1Hi,jRγiγj

    It has one zero mode (MZM), N positive-energy states, and N negative-energy states.

  • Particle-Hole Symmetry Constraint: The MZM ϕM satisfies PϕM=ϕM where P=K (complex conjugation). This forces the MZM wavefunction to be real, obeying Gaussian orthogonal ensemble (GOE) statistics. In contrast, CdGM eigenspinors ϕCdGM are complex, obeying Gaussian unitary ensemble (GUE) statistics.

Key Innovations ​

  1. Real vs Complex Wavefunction Distinction: The only fundamental difference between MZM and CdGM wavefunctions in the disordered RMT limit is that the former is real while the latter is complex. This leads to distinct probability density distributions for squared components w=|ϕ(1)|2:

    fM(w)=1πΓ(D/2)Γ((D−1)/2)(1−w)(D−3)/2wfCdGM(w)=(D−1)(1−w)D−2

    where D=2N+1.

  2. Variance Ratio of 2: For the squared wavefunction components in the Majorana basis, the variances satisfy:

    Var(wM)=2(D−1)D2(D+2)≈2D2,Var(wCdGM)=D−1D2(D+1)≈1D2

    Thus Var(wM)/Var(wCdGM)→2 as D→∞.

  3. IPR Ratio of 4/3 in Nambu Basis: Transforming to the Nambu basis, the total probability density ρ(x→)=|u(x→)|2+|v(x→)|2 has second moments:

    ⟨ρM2⟩≈8D2,⟨ρCdGM2⟩≈6D2

    yielding ⟨ρM2⟩/⟨ρCdGM2⟩≈4/3. This maps directly to the IPR measured in STM experiments.

  4. Experimental Feasibility: The authors estimate that under optimal binary hypothesis testing, only ∼83 independent spatial measurements are theoretically sufficient to distinguish the two distributions at <5% error rate.

Technical Approach ​

STM Tunneling Conductance: The differential conductance G(x→,V)≡dI/dV is related to the local density of states (LDOS):

G(x→,V)=G0β4πℏN1(μ0)∫0∞dEcosh−2⁡[(E−μ0−eV)β2]∫−∞∞dE′ℏNs(x→,E′)Im1E′−E+μ0−iη

where the LDOS is Ns(x→,E)=∑En≥0|un(x→)|2δ(E−En)+|vn(x→)|2δ(E+En).

Symmetrized Conductance: To extract the total probability density, the symmetrized conductance Gsym(x→,V)=G(x→,V)+G(x→,−V) is used. In the low-temperature limit and when tunneling resolves a single mode:

limη→0∑x→Gsym2(x→,0)∑x→Gsym2(x→,En/e)≈⟨ρM2⟩⟨ρn2⟩

Numerical Simulation: A 2D spinless px+ipy superconductor on a square lattice is simulated using Kwant. Parameters: lattice constant a=25 nm, μ=100 μeV, hopping t≈400 μeV, pairing Δ0=100 μeV. A stadium-shaped antidot (L=60a, R=50a) with on-site disorder uniformly distributed in [−Δ0,Δ0] breaks spatial symmetries and ensures quantum chaos.

Normalized IPR: Within the stadium region S:

Inorm(ρ)=∑x→∈Sρ(x→)2[∑x→∈Sρ(x→)]2

with predictions NsiteInorm(ρM)≈2 and NsiteInorm(ρCdGM)≈3/2.

Main Results ​

RMT Variance Scaling (Fig. 2):

  • Numerical simulations over 20,000 random matrix realizations for D=101 to 601 confirm the analytical variance scaling.
  • The variance ratio Var(wM)/Var(wCdGM) converges to 2 as the sample size increases.

Kwant Numerical Verification (Fig. 3):

  • Analysis of the 20 lowest positive-energy states across 104 disorder realizations yields a mean ratio of scaled IPRs of 1.36±0.11, in agreement with the theoretical prediction of 4/3.
  • The MZM density distribution for a single realization is shown in the inset.

Probability Density Histograms (Fig. 4):

  • For a single disorder realization sampled over the whole stadium (13,765 sites), the CdGM-to-MZM variance ratio is 1.83, approaching 2.
  • For a uniform subsampled region (459 sites), the ratio is 1.29.
  • The histograms agree well with the theoretical PDFs in Eqs. (10) and (11).

Spectral Resolution Requirement:

  • Poor resolution admixes MZM and CdGM contributions. The measured IPR then interpolates between the two limits and should never exceed the universal upper bound of 4/3. Equal admixture yields a weighted average of 7/6.

Reading Notes ​

  • [ ] RMT Derivation: Study the detailed derivation of the probability density functions fM(w) and fCdGM(w) for GOE and GUE eigenvector components, and how the Nambu-basis transformation preserves the variance ratio.
  • [ ] Experimental Parameters: What are the realistic values of disorder strength, antidot size, and temperature in current TI-SC heterostructure experiments? Can the required spectral resolution be achieved?
  • [ ] Chernoff Bound: The ∼83 measurement lower bound assumes optimal binary hypothesis testing — what are the practical limitations of this estimate for real STM setups?
  • [ ] Beyond Single Vortex: How do these statistics generalize to systems with multiple MZMs or vortex-vortex interactions?
  • [ ] Comparison with Other Platforms: Can similar statistical signatures be engineered in nanowire-based MZMs or Fe-based superconductor vortices?

Issue status: 🟢 Open (accepting more papers)
